items.q: Why does the left ventricle have a thicker muscular wall than the right?
items.correct: It must pump blood at high pressure around the whole body; the right ventricle only pumps to the nearby lungs.
items.misconception: The left ventricle is thicker because it handles oxygenated blood, which is harder to pump.
items.random_wrong: The left ventricle is thicker because it is on the same side as the brain.
items.why: Students focus on blood type (oxygenated vs not) rather than the pressure needed to push blood around the systemic circuit.

items.q: How are signals transmitted across a synapse between two neurones?
items.correct: Via neurotransmitter chemicals released from the presynaptic neurone, which bind receptors on the postsynaptic neurone.
items.misconception: The electrical signal jumps directly from one neurone to the next across the synaptic gap.
items.random_wrong: Neurones touch each other at synapses and pass current through direct contact.
items.why: Students think of nerves as 'wires' carrying electricity straight through, missing the chemical step at each synapse.

items.q: Why is a reflex action faster than a conscious response?
items.correct: The signal does not need to be processed by the brain — it travels through a simpler reflex arc via the spinal cord.
items.misconception: The brain decides reflexes faster than other actions because it skips thinking.
items.random_wrong: Reflexes are faster because they use specially fast neurones not used elsewhere.
items.why: Students assume the brain is always in charge; they don't realise reflexes specifically bypass the brain to save time.

items.q: Is the blood in your veins actually blue?
items.correct: No — blood is always red; deoxygenated blood is just a darker red. Veins only look blue through the skin.
items.misconception: Blood is blue in the veins and turns red when it meets air.
items.random_wrong: Blood has no colour until it is oxygenated.
items.why: Diagrams colour veins blue and skin scatters light, but haemoglobin keeps all blood red, dark red when deoxygenated and bright red when oxygenated.

items.q: Do all arteries carry oxygenated blood and all veins carry deoxygenated blood?
items.correct: No — the pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood and the pulmonary vein carries oxygenated blood.
items.misconception: Arteries always carry oxygenated blood; veins always carry deoxygenated blood.
items.random_wrong: Arteries and veins carry the same mixed blood everywhere.
items.why: Arteries and veins are defined by direction (away from or toward the heart), not oxygen content; the pulmonary vessels are the key exceptions.

items.q: Which heart chamber pumps oxygenated blood out to the body?
items.why: Correct answer: Left ventricle. “Right atrium” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which blood vessel has walls just ONE cell thick?
items.why: Correct answer: Capillary. “Artery” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many chambers does a human heart have in total?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
